course_info.title: Visual Design Fundamentals
prerequisites: UX101 - Introduction to User Experience Design

learning_objectives: Apply the principles of visual hierarchy to guide user attention
Use size, color, contrast, and position to establish importance
Analyze existing designs for their visual hierarchy effectiveness
readings: Universal Principles of Design by Lidwell, Holden & Butler, entries on Hierarchy and Gestalt
Refactoring UI by Adam Wathan & Steve Schoger, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Visual design is not decoration — it is a communication system. Visual hierarchy determines the order in which users process information on a screen.
key_concepts: Visual hierarchy: size, color, contrast, position, whitespace
F-pattern and Z-pattern reading behavior on screens
Gestalt principles: proximity, similarity, continuity, closure, figure-ground
The squint test for evaluating hierarchy at a glance
Contrast ratios and their role in both hierarchy and accessibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Side-by-side comparison of landing pages with strong vs. weak visual hierarchy, with class identifying what draws the eye first and why

review.value: The arrangement of elements to guide the viewer's eye through content in order of importance — achieved through size, color, contrast, spacing, and positioning
Elements placed near each other are perceived as related — the single most powerful principle for organizing information in interface design

learning_objectives: Select and pair typefaces for readability and personality
Apply a typographic scale to create consistent text hierarchy
Set appropriate line height, letter spacing, and measure for screen reading
readings: Thinking with Type by Ellen Lupton, Ch. 1-2
Butterick's Practical Typography, online chapters on screen typography

lecture_notes.introduction: Typography accounts for approximately 95% of web design. Choosing and configuring type is the single most impactful visual design decision you will make.
key_concepts: Typeface anatomy: x-height, baseline, ascenders, descenders
Serif vs. sans-serif for screen readability
Typographic scale: mathematical ratios for heading/body sizes
Line height (leading): 1.4-1.6x for body text on screen
Measure (line length): 45-75 characters for optimal readability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live redesign of a text-heavy web page applying typographic principles — before and after comparison in Figma
setup_requirements: Figma with type scale plugin installed

review.value: A mathematical progression of font sizes (e.g., 1.25 ratio: 12, 15, 18.75, 23.4, 29.3) that creates harmonious, consistent visual hierarchy across headings and body text
45-75 characters per line for body text — too wide and the eye loses its place returning to the next line; too narrow and reading becomes choppy

learning_objectives: Build a functional color palette for a digital product
Apply color psychology to support brand and user emotion
Ensure color choices meet WCAG accessibility contrast requirements
readings: Interaction of Color by Josef Albers, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Color in UI design serves three purposes: establishing visual hierarchy, communicating meaning (success, error, warning), and expressing brand personality. Every color choice must serve at least one of these functions.
key_concepts: HSL color model for designers: hue, saturation, lightness
Building a palette: primary, secondary, neutral, semantic colors
60-30-10 rule for color distribution
WCAG contrast ratios: 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text
Color blindness considerations: 8% of males have red-green deficiency
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete UI color palette from a single brand color using HSL manipulation in Figma, then testing for accessibility
setup_requirements: Figma with color palette generator plugin
Color blindness simulation tool (Stark plugin or Sim Daltonism)

review.value: 60% dominant neutral color, 30% secondary color, 10% accent/primary color — prevents visual chaos and maintains hierarchy
Minimum 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18pt+) — ensures readability for users with low vision

learning_objectives: Construct responsive grid systems for web and mobile layouts
Apply spacing systems using an 8-point grid for consistency
Design layouts that adapt gracefully across screen sizes
readings: Grid Systems in Graphic Design by Josef Muller-Brockmann, Ch. 1-3
Refactoring UI by Adam Wathan & Steve Schoger, Ch. on Layout and Spacing

lecture_notes.introduction: Grid systems provide the invisible structure that makes interfaces feel organized and professional. A consistent spatial system eliminates arbitrary decisions and speeds up design.
key_concepts: Column grids: 12-column systems and their flexibility
8-point spatial grid: all spacing and sizing in multiples of 8
Responsive breakpoints: mobile, tablet, desktop, wide screen
Content-first layout: letting content dictate structure
Whitespace as a design element, not empty space
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a responsive dashboard layout in Figma using auto-layout and a 12-column grid system with 8px spacing

review.value: All spacing, sizing, and positioning use multiples of 8px — creates visual rhythm, simplifies design decisions, and aligns with most device pixel densities
Flexible grid that divides into halves, thirds, quarters, and sixths — the standard foundation for responsive web layouts

learning_objectives: Design and select icons that are universally recognizable
Apply consistent icon styling rules across an interface
Use imagery effectively to support content without distraction
readings: The Icon Handbook by Jon Hicks, Ch. 1-3
Material Design Guidelines: Iconography and Imagery sections

lecture_notes.introduction: Icons are visual shorthand. When they work, they speed comprehension. When they fail, they create confusion. Learning to design and select effective icons is an essential visual design skill.
key_concepts: Icon recognition: metaphor, convention, and cultural context
Icon grid systems: optical alignment within a consistent bounding box
Stroke vs. filled icons: when to use each style
Icon + label: when icons need text labels for clarity
Photography and illustration: selecting imagery that supports the design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Icon design exercise in Figma: creating a set of 6 consistent icons for a navigation bar using a shared grid and style rules
setup_requirements: Icon reference libraries (Phosphor Icons, Material Icons)

review.value: Universal metaphors (magnifying glass = search) > learned conventions (hamburger menu) > arbitrary symbols (need text labels) — always test icon comprehension with users
A consistent bounding box and keyline grid ensures all icons appear the same visual weight and size, even when their shapes differ

class_schedule.topic: Visual Design Systems and Brand Application
learning_objectives: Build a cohesive visual design system with reusable components
Document design tokens (colors, typography, spacing) for developer handoff
Apply brand guidelines consistently across multiple screens
readings: Design Systems by Alla Kholmatova, Ch. 1-3
Atomic Design by Brad Frost, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Individual design decisions mean nothing without consistency. A visual design system codifies your decisions into reusable patterns that scale across an entire product.
key_concepts: Design tokens: the smallest units of a design system (colors, fonts, spacing values)
Atomic design hierarchy: atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, pages
Component variants and states (default, hover, active, disabled, error)
Documentation for developer handoff: specs, redlines, token names
Brand application: translating brand identity into interface design patterns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a mini design system in Figma: creating a token library, then assembling atoms into molecules into a complete card component
setup_requirements: Figma with component and style features

review.value: Named values representing visual design decisions (e.g., color-primary: #2563EB, spacing-md: 16px) — the bridge between design and code
Methodology organizing UI elements in increasing complexity: atoms (button), molecules (search bar), organisms (header), templates (page layout), pages (final implementation)

questions.question: A landing page has three equally sized headings, a paragraph of body text in 11px font, and no whitespace between sections. Identify at least three visual design problems and explain how to fix each.
questions.answer: Problems: (1) Three equally sized headings create no visual hierarchy — fix by using a typographic scale with distinct sizes for H1, H2, H3. (2) 11px body text is below the recommended minimum of 16px for screen readability — increase to at least 16px. (3) No whitespace between sections means content runs together with no visual separation — add consistent spacing using an 8-point grid system between sections.

rubric.key: Identifies readability problem with font size
Provides specific, actionable fixes for each

questions.question: Explain the Gestalt principle of proximity and describe how it applies to form design. Give a specific example.
questions.answer: The Gestalt principle of proximity states that elements placed close together are perceived as a related group. In form design, this means related fields (like First Name and Last Name) should be visually grouped together with less spacing between them and more spacing between different field groups (like personal info vs. shipping address). For example, in a checkout form, the Street, City, State, and ZIP fields should be grouped tightly, with a larger gap before the Payment section begins.

questions.question: A designer proposes using red text (#FF0000) on a dark blue background (#1E3A5F) for an important warning message. Evaluate this choice from both a visual design and accessibility perspective.
questions.answer: From an accessibility perspective, this fails WCAG guidelines — the contrast ratio of #FF0000 on #1E3A5F is approximately 3.1:1, below the 4.5:1 minimum for normal text. From a visual design perspective, red and blue are complementary colors that create visual vibration when placed together, causing eye strain. Additionally, approximately 8% of males have red-green color blindness, making red less reliable as the sole warning indicator. Fix: use a lighter red or orange on the dark background to meet contrast ratios, add a warning icon alongside the text, and do not rely on color alone to convey meaning.

rubric.key: Identifies contrast ratio failure with approximate ratio

questions.question: Explain the 8-point grid system and describe two benefits it provides for both designers and developers.
questions.answer: The 8-point grid system means all spacing, sizing, and positioning values are multiples of 8 pixels (8, 16, 24, 32, 40, etc.). Benefits for designers: (1) Eliminates arbitrary spacing decisions — every spacing choice comes from a consistent scale, creating visual rhythm and reducing design time. (2) Creates a shared language with developers — instead of debating pixel values, both teams reference the same scale. Benefits for developers: (1) Aligns with most device pixel densities — 8 divides evenly into common screen resolutions, avoiding sub-pixel rendering issues. (2) Simplifies responsive scaling — consistent spacing scales predictably across breakpoints.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of the 8-point grid system

major_assignments.title: Visual Redesign Project
major_assignments.description: Select an existing website or app with poor visual design and create a comprehensive visual redesign. Apply all principles covered in class — hierarchy, typography, color, layout, and iconography — to transform the experience while maintaining the original content and functionality.
objectives: Analyze existing visual design problems systematically
Build a complete visual design system with tokens and components
Apply typography, color, and layout principles to solve real problems
Present design decisions with clear rationale
deliverables: Visual audit of the original design (3-5 pages) documenting specific problems
Design system: color palette, type scale, spacing system, icon set
High-fidelity redesigns of at least 3 key screens in Figma
Before/after comparison presentation with design rationale

criteria: Original problems identified accurately with evidence
Problems categorized by type (hierarchy, typography, color, layout)
Severity and user impact assessed for each problem
Audit is thorough and professionally documented
Color palette meets accessibility contrast requirements
Type scale is mathematically consistent and well-paired
Spacing system follows 8-point grid consistently
All tokens are clearly named and documented
Strong visual hierarchy guides the eye correctly
Typography is readable and well-configured for screen
Color usage is purposeful and consistent
Layout is responsive-ready with proper grid alignment
Clear before/after comparisons for each screen
Design decisions are grounded in principles taught in class
Rationale is persuasive and evidence-based
Presentation is well-structured and professional
Thorough visual audit identifying real problems with evidence
Complete and accessible design system with documented tokens
High-quality redesigns demonstrating mastery of visual principles
Compelling presentation connecting design decisions to principles

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on visual design principles, typography, color theory, and layout

design_exercises.description: Weekly hands-on exercises applying individual visual design skills in Figma

visual_redesign_project.description: Comprehensive redesign project applying all visual design principles to a real product

design_critiques.description: Written and verbal critiques of peers' work using design vocabulary and principles

final_design_system.description: Complete visual design system with documentation suitable for developer handoff

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Studio sessions include peer critique and cannot be replicated independently. More than 3 absences will affect your participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Design exercises are due at the start of class for peer critique. Late exercises receive no critique feedback and lose 15% per day. The redesign project loses 10% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer critique and discussion are core to this course. Final deliverables must be individual work. Sharing Figma files for copying is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs must be original work. Using UI kits or pre-made templates without disclosure and significant modification is considered academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Figma (free education plan), WebAIM contrast checker, access to Google Fonts, and a color blindness simulator (Stark plugin or equivalent).
